title: Fei Fei Li on Putting Humans at the Center of AI
text:
Hi everybody. My name is Sebastian.
I'm the founder of Udacity and I have with me a really,
really good friend and colleague from Stanford University, Professor Fei-Fei Li.
Hi, I'm Fei-Fei.
Fei-Fei joined Stanford about 10 years ago.
Yes. Good memory.
Yes, and I was involved in recruiting her from Princeton.
Many phone calls from you.
It is possibly the best thing I ever did at Stanford to get Fei-Fei Li
into Stanford who is now a distinguished chair professor,
she has the Sequoia chair.
She's also a co-director of the Human Centered-AI Institute,
and she just won a 10 year award for the most important past paper in
computer vision for building
a massive dataset that has propelled the field more than almost anything else.
So it's a great honor to know you.
Honor is mine. I was just saying that how wonderful
it was coming into Stanford AI Lab when you are still the director.
It's a long time ago.
I followed your footstep.
Good.
Let's talk about artificial intelligence because you saw at the pulse of it.
You've seen it from the academic side and you've seen it from the industry side,
how do you feel from the academic side, what's different?
So for those of us, you included, Sebastian,
we entered AI during what people call AI winter time.
So I remembered I started my PhD studying AI in the year of 2000 where
the public hasn't even heard of this word but it was purely
an intellectual curiosity for myself to get into this field.
Well, 20 years later or 19 years later,
it is an absolutely complete change.
The field has gone from academic niche,
tackled in underneath the computer science discipline into
a vast field of many different sub-fields as was just its impact.
So whether you're in academia or industry,
you've got to feel this,
it's just a unique time now.
For a long time you've worked on making computers smart and the ability to see the world,
computer vision as it's called.
Then at some point, you flip over and said,
"Now we're going to work on human-centered AI"? What happened?
I don't think that's a continuum.
It's definitely not a flip over.
So what happens is that
my academic research area is still the intersection between machine learning,
deep learning, and computer vision.
But around the time in 2015,
I gave a TED Talk.
Which I attended.
Yeah, and that's a couple of years after your TED Talk.
I'm just sharing with the world that there is a change
coming in the technology of computer vision and during that TED Talk,
I talked about the potential applications.
But I don't think many of us predicted how fast that change start to come to industry.
So 2017, 2018, I took a sabbatical from Stanford and was working as
the chief scientist of AI at Google Cloud where I was overseeing the enterprise AI,
R&D products and business solutions,
and one of the biggest things I learned there and
it continues to illuminate me until today,
is how much AI is transforming businesses and it's touching
everyone's life and will be in such a profound way.
Sometimes we don't feel it,
but it's there whether you're shopping online,
or going through healthcare system and thinking about diagnosis,
and medication, and treatment,
or transportation, or anything.
So with that in mind,
I think it really dawned on me around
2017 that this is a technology that is about humans,
that there's no independent value of AI.
AI's value should be human values.
When that dawned on me,
I start to develop a new framework in my mind about how we should advance
AI and deploy AI in the future and they should be under a human-centered framework.
You say AI is about human values,
do you believe an AI system has to have values?
I don't think technology has independent values.
I think from the design development to deployment,
the technology carry our values.
Then some people say AI will just eradicate all jobs.
This is like some Altman's, I guess perspective.
Where on that spectrum do we end up?
Silicon Valley, many of us probably believe that
the best way to predict the future is to create the future, right?
Yeah. [inaudible].
So I really believe this technology like many technology has
bigger potential to enhance humans and augment humans rather than replace humans.
It's more than a little bit,
it's on us is how we want to design this.
But I want to give you a concrete example.
For the past eight years,
I've been working in healthcare.
Sebastian, you're a pioneer in self-driving car.
I remember when I joined Stanford,
Stanley was still at Stanford, right?
Correct.
You went to Google and start paving the way to show the world the combination
of smart sensors that has a holistic view of the world and the environment,
plus powerful algorithms behind these data since data
can help make critical decisions and assist mobility.
The same thing is going to happen in healthcare and that's
my example of how we can be assistive of healthcare delivery.
Our clinicians, American clinicians,
whether we're talking about nurses and doctors are overworked,
overwhelmed, and the amount of physician and
nurse attrition and dropout rate is all-time high.
In the meantime, our patients are not getting enough care in their treatment.
We have a huge error rate in medical delivery that's killing people.
I don't know if you know that just hospital-acquired infection,
which is the number 3 or 2 [inaudible] killer of
American patients in hospital claims more lives,
almost three times more lives than car accidents per year in our country.
What can we do? It's a very difficult problem to
ensure the kind of healthcare delivery quality under certain controlled cost.
Well, smart sensors and computer vision can come to help.
We've been collaborating with
Stanford Children's Hospital and Utah's Intermountain Hospital
to put cheap sensors around a hospital ward
and help to monitor clinicians' hand-hygiene practice.
That is a critical component of controlling hospital-acquired infection
and pioneering and designing a system to
remind the doctors or
nurses when they need to wash their hands before they touch the patients.
That's just one tiny example.
We're also doing work in ICUs to help monitor
patients' mobility while moving patients in clinically safe way helps their recovery.
There are also unsafe moments when patients are delirious and so on,
and we put sensors to help continuously monitor the situation of patients.
So these are examples that AI is there to augment and
to enhance the service we can do for people,
not to replace people.
This is very interesting. Layout to me the future of 20,
30, 50 years in medicine.
What do you think the AI will do to medicine in general to help services?
So I think there's a lot of
exciting changes coming and one of my good friends, Eric Topol,
has written a very interesting book called
Deep Medicine that shows AI's potential impact,
and I think there are several areas.
One is that with the digitization of EMR,
EHR, electronic hospital records and electronic medical record,
there is a lot of opportunity to take those data analytics to help
move from posterior treatment to predictive help to our patients.
Also, because of the huge throughput of data collected in medicine these days,
whether it's radiology or pathology and other physiological data,
we are looking at AI's help in diagnosis,
in treatment plan, in democratizing and making
this cheaper and more accessible to different parts of the global population.
It's heartbreaking to know that while in Silicon Valley
we have the access to the best technology in medicine
and many parts of this country as well as the world people
are still having a hard time accessing doctors and the healthcare,
and hopefully, AI can help with that.
Last but not the least, I think healthcare,
I personally focus on the verb care in healthcare.
So whether we're talking about this huge uptake in aging population or talking
about just caring for patients in-patient or in homes and rehabilitation and all that,
AI can come to help as whether it's
a guardian angel or ambient intelligence or assistive technology.
So tell me about yourself.
You started out your career at Princeton,
you moved over to Caltech for your Masters and your PhD,
and back to Princeton.
You obviously did it as a minority in an environment.
In CS.
In Computer Science, there were not that
many women around at the time and neither are today.
What were your struggles? What did you learn?
What were the moments that defined you?
Good question, Sebastian.
I started as a physics major in Princeton,
and a lot of students ask me how early should they start Computer Science.
My answer is, "You should start knowing
what your passion is and pursue that as early as you can,
but it doesn't have to be Computer Science."
Many paths lead to AI, for example,
and I want to talk a little bit later about why
I see AI as an interdisciplinary field later.
So I was extremely interested in the mystery
of the universe and the origin of the universe that the distant
galaxies and the subatomic particles till I started reading books
about great physicists in the 20th century like Einstein and Schrodinger,
and noticed that their own interest in the later part of their life
shifted from the atomic world to the human biological world.
There's more questions and mysteries about life itself.
Then I get particularly interested in intelligence,
and that's how I decided my PhD will be on
simultaneously human cognitive neuroscience as well as AI.
At that time, we don't call it AI,
we call it Computer Vision and Machine Learning.
Early struggle as a grad student is actually traversing
that interdisciplinary research because I
was simultaneously doing work in neuroscience and AI
and it's easy to fall through the crack, you know that.
You have to be doing impactful work in
both fields but also combine them in interesting ways,
and I feel lucky that I had amazing environment at Caltech.
Christof Koch, Pietro Perona,
these are my advisors who work with me closely on this work.
Then I went to Princeton as a Assistant Professor.
I think there, one of the struggle is really believing,
wanting to do something impactful,
but have that grit to carry through when the world doesn't believe that,
and that happened to the image in that project where we
saw very early in 2006 that machine learning was stuck in
a local minimum because we're all fiddling with these parameters of
a small Bayesian network model while we might have to think entirely differently,
thinking about data-driven machine learning and how that can reboot
the way we can train computer algorithms.
But that mindset or that idea is not exactly mainstream at that time,
and especially wanting to demonstrate that on a data set crazily huge,
we were downloading billions of images from the Internet and wanting to
put together the world's largest data set of everything,
it's tens of thousands of object classes and tens of millions of images.
That process was scary hindsight.
Not much effort but luckily, we succeeded.
It feels like from today's perspective,
you would not be anywhere close in
the eye without that work that you did back in the day.
Thank you.
I can commiserate that, at the time,
I think people love publishing algorithms and they didn't love publishing data sets.
Yeah.
So how you kept yourself motivated doing this?
Well, you see on the horizon or even beyond the horizon, the eventual goal.
We were very clear.
My student and I,
we somehow had a very clear vision,
this is not about data set,
this is about rebooting AI algorithms.
With that kind of goal on the horizon,
you have to just be resilient.
I would say earlier,
the same thing is happening in our healthcare research that we see the importance of
ambient intelligence in helping health care delivery and be assistive.
But to get all the proper legal approval,
IRB approval, specking out the right sensors,
getting the hospital to just put a screw on the sensors and hanging on the wall,
that process takes years and it's the resilience that researchers have to
have or entrepreneurs have to have to get something done.
Let's talk about human-centered AI.
Obviously, you started this together with John Etchemendy,
the former provost and logic expert from Humanity.
Yes, philosopher.
Philosopher. What's wrong about AI?
Why we even need this center? What is it good for?
That's a great question.
When we started this, we were laughing about what's wrong with AI,
but I want to say what's changed in AI.
AI is a field that's more or less 60 years old,
and I think it has come to a phase change.
I fundamentally believe AI is no longer a Computer Science discipline,
it's a interdisciplinary field.
When I say interdisciplinary,
I don't mean just to engineering field,
I mean it's a very wide field
of study that should include Humanities and Social Sciences.
Let me lay out the three founding principles of human-centered AI framework that,
at Stanford, our institute is built on and we believe in.
The first principle is betting on
the future of AI technology should be more human-inspired.
Here, I'm inviting the cross-pollination of brain science,
neuroscience, cognitive science with AI.
So whether we're looking at the deep,
algorithm level, new learning rules,
or we're talking about the cognitively inspired new tasks,
it's a spectrum of possibilities to drive
the next-generation AI technology to be human-inspired,
that's the first principle.
This requires our students to be versed in a larger set
of understanding of cognition,
or neuroscience, or applied physics in addition to Computer Science.
Second human-centered AI principle is
to rethink AI as a Humanities and Social Science field,
not just Computer Science field.
Here, we're inviting thinkers and students to study economics,
study history, study law,
study ethics, study political science in the context
of AI so that we can answer questions like future of work,
like machine learning fairness and bias,
like ethical codes of AI,
like geopolitical implications of AI,
and all these social and human impact.
The goal here is that we want to understand and anticipate AI's impact.
Hopefully, Stanford also has an aspiration to become a partner to policymakers,
to recommend thoughtful policies of how AI will impact our life.
Third which is our last principle, but not the least,
goes back to what we talked about earlier,
Sebastian, is we want to change a verb.
The verb that's associated with AI today is replaced,
but we really believe the opportunity and potential
that more resources should put in designing,
developing, and deploying human-centered AI technology that augments and enhance humans,
not to replace humans.
Here, Stanford is particularly passionate about fields like education,
healthcare, automation and manufacturing, and sustainability.
So these are the three founding principles that we want to
instill here at Stanford to reboot AI into
an interdisciplinary field where we'll have classes
for students that are in cross-departments and schools.
For example, one of the most popular classes that was offered
just this past quarter was a technology
ethics class co-taught by a political science and philosophy professor,
a Computer Science professor,
and students come all over the campus to take that class.
I also taught a class last quarter with
a behavioral psychologist from the Business School on AI and human
well-being thinking about how to design from a business and application point of view,
technology that enhances human dignity and well-being.
So these are the new changes we want to see.
Nice. Last question, you happen to be very successfully married
to one of your colleagues in computer vision,
what are your dinner conversations like?
We still have young kids so you can imagine is, "Eat your vegetable."
But my husband, Silvio Savarese, is a roboticist.
I'm also personally very excited by the future of
AI and robotics so we talk a lot about this.
But truthfully, we have a lot of normal family conversations.
Well, Fei-Fei, it's great to have you here.
Thank you, Sebastian.
I hope everyone who watches this enjoys.
Thank you.
She's by far one of the smartest people I've ever met.
The feeling is mutual.
Honestly, when we interviewed her,
I'm a little bit older than she is obviously,
she gave the best answer to the question,
how to shape your research into world impact?
Thank you.
Which is something that many academics don't know how to answer.
Thank you.
Fei-Fei, great having you over.
Thank you, Sebastian.
